meanings.definition: Serving to restore strength or vitality; restorative
meanings.example: The physician prescribed an analeptic regimen of rest and nourishing broths.

meanings.definition: Of, relating to, or acting as a restorative stimulant of the central nervous system
meanings.example: Caffeine is one of the most widely consumed analeptic compounds in the world.

etymology: From Greek analēptikos 'restorative', from analambanein 'to take up'

meanings.definition: An item of food; something fit to be eaten
meanings.example: The market stall displayed an impressive array of comestibles from across the Mediterranean.

meanings.definition: Any article of food or nourishment prepared or available for human consumption
meanings.example: The survival expert surveyed the forest plants to identify which could serve as viable comestibles.

etymology: From Medieval Latin comestibilis, from Latin comestus, past participle of comedere 'to eat up'

meanings.definition: The state of being excessively fat or obese
meanings.example: His growing corpulence was a source of concern for his physician.

meanings.definition: A quality of bulk or heaviness in physical form, sometimes depicted in literature as a sign of indulgence or lack of restraint
meanings.example: The portrait captured the king's corpulence with unflattering accuracy, making it a pointed commentary on court excess.

etymology: From Latin corpulentia, from corpulentus 'fleshy', from corpus 'body'

meanings.definition: To deprive of force, strength, or vitality; to weaken.
meanings.example: The relentless tropical heat seemed to enervate even the most energetic members of the expedition.

meanings.definition: To reduce the mental or moral vigour of someone.
meanings.example: Years of bureaucratic routine had enervated his once-ambitious spirit.

etymology: From Latin 'enervare' (to weaken), from 'ex-' (out of) + 'nervus' (sinew, nerve).

meanings.definition: Hard to control, manage, or resolve; stubbornly resistant to treatment or correction.
meanings.example: The intractable conflict between the two factions had persisted for generations.

meanings.definition: (Of a person) Difficult to deal with; stubborn or uncooperative.
meanings.example: The committee found the witness intractable and unwilling to answer straightforward questions.

etymology: From Latin 'intractabilis', from 'in-' (not) + 'tractabilis' (manageable), from 'tractare' (to handle).

meanings.definition: Difficult to understand; obscure or esoteric in nature
meanings.example: The professor's abstruse lecture on quantum chromodynamics left most students bewildered.

meanings.definition: Concealed or hidden from easy perception
meanings.example: The abstruse symbolism in the medieval manuscript required years of scholarly interpretation.

etymology: From Latin abstrusus 'hidden, concealed', past participle of abstrudere 'to push away'

meanings.definition: Characterized by severe self-discipline and abstention from indulgence
meanings.example: The monk's ascetic lifestyle included fasting, silence, and sleeping on a bare wooden floor.

meanings.definition: Practicing or characteristic of extreme self-discipline and renunciation of sensory pleasures
meanings.example: The ascetic practices of early Christian hermits profoundly shaped later monastic traditions.

etymology: From Greek askētikos 'rigorously self-disciplined', from askein 'to exercise'

meanings.definition: Abrupt or offhand in speech or manner; curtly blunt
meanings.example: The surgeon's brusque bedside manner unnerved patients, though his skills were unmatched.

etymology: From French brusque, from Italian brusco 'sour, rough'

meanings.definition: Showing a cruel disregard for others; emotionally insensitive
meanings.example: The CEO's callous dismissal of the layoffs angered employees and shareholders alike.

meanings.definition: Hardened or thickened, especially of skin
meanings.example: Years of manual labour had left his hands callous and rough to the touch.

etymology: From Latin callosus 'thick-skinned', from callum 'hard skin'

meanings.definition: In keeping with good taste and propriety; polite and restrained
meanings.example: The ambassador maintained a decorous composure throughout the tense diplomatic reception.

meanings.definition: Characterized by dignified propriety in conduct and appearance
meanings.example: The decorous furnishings of the Victorian parlour reflected the family's emphasis on respectability.

etymology: From Latin decorus 'seemly, becoming', from decor 'beauty, grace'

meanings.definition: The art of investigating or discussing the truth of opinions through logical argumentation
meanings.example: Socratic dialectic proceeds by questioning assumptions until contradictions are revealed.

meanings.definition: The tension or opposition between two interacting forces or elements
meanings.example: The dialectic between tradition and innovation drives much of contemporary architectural design.

etymology: From Greek dialektikē (tekhnē) '(art) of debate', from dialegesthai 'to converse'

meanings.definition: To involve someone deeply in a conflict, argument, or difficult situation
meanings.example: The leaked documents embroiled the corporation in a protracted legal battle.

meanings.definition: To bring into a state of confusion or disorder
meanings.example: Rival factions embroiled the parliament in chaos, delaying legislation for months.

etymology: From French embrouiller 'to entangle, confuse', from en- 'in' + brouiller 'to mix up'

meanings.definition: Not showing a serious or respectful attitude; inappropriately casual
meanings.example: His flippant remarks during the funeral service offended everyone in attendance.

meanings.definition: Lacking gravity or respect for serious things; marked by an inappropriate lightness of tone
meanings.example: His flippant response to the grieving family's questions drew widespread condemnation in the press.

etymology: Probably related to flip, suggesting a tossing or careless manner; first attested in the 17th century

meanings.definition: Sly or cunning intelligence used to deceive or manipulate
meanings.example: Through sheer guile, the negotiator secured terms that no one thought possible.

meanings.definition: The use of clever but dishonest behaviour to achieve a goal
meanings.example: The fox in the fable relied on guile rather than strength to outwit the lion.

etymology: From Old French guile 'deceit, trickery', probably of Germanic origin

meanings.definition: Overly theatrical or melodramatic in character or style
meanings.example: The witness's histrionic outburst in court undermined the credibility of her testimony.

meanings.definition: Relating to actors or acting
meanings.example: His histrionic talents were evident even in childhood, when he would stage elaborate performances for the family.

etymology: From Latin histrionicus 'of an actor', from histrio 'actor'

meanings.definition: Not resulting from or achieved through deliberate planning; unintentional
meanings.example: The inadvertent release of confidential data prompted an immediate internal investigation.

meanings.definition: Failing to act carefully or considerately; inattentive
meanings.example: Her inadvertent disregard for the filing deadline cost the firm a significant penalty.

etymology: From Latin inadvertens, from in- 'not' + advertere 'to turn toward'

meanings.definition: Not in harmony or keeping with the surroundings or other aspects of something
meanings.example: The ultra-modern glass tower looked incongruous amid the Victorian terrace houses.

meanings.definition: Not in accordance with what is logical, expected, or appropriate
meanings.example: His incongruous laughter during the solemn ceremony drew disapproving stares.

etymology: From Latin incongruus, from in- 'not' + congruus 'agreeing, suitable'

meanings.definition: Wanting to avoid activity or exertion; lazy
meanings.example: The indolent heir showed no interest in managing the family estate.

meanings.definition: (Of a disease or condition) causing little or no pain and progressing slowly
meanings.example: The biopsy revealed an indolent lymphoma that required only routine monitoring.

etymology: From Late Latin indolentem 'insensitive to pain', from in- 'not' + dolere 'to grieve'

meanings.definition: Tending to obstruct or harm; hostile
meanings.example: The new regulations proved inimical to small businesses struggling to comply.

meanings.definition: Unfriendly or showing active opposition
meanings.example: The two nations had maintained inimical relations for decades before the peace accord.

etymology: From Late Latin inimicalis, from Latin inimicus 'enemy', from in- 'not' + amicus 'friend'

meanings.definition: Not harmful or offensive; unlikely to provoke a strong reaction
meanings.example: What seemed like an innocuous remark at dinner escalated into a heated family argument.

meanings.definition: Not interesting or stimulating; bland
meanings.example: The committee produced an innocuous report that carefully avoided all controversial topics.

etymology: From Latin innocuus, from in- 'not' + nocuus 'harmful', from nocere 'to hurt'

meanings.definition: Lacking flavour; tasteless
meanings.example: The hotel's insipid breakfast buffet left much to be desired after a week of travel.

meanings.definition: Lacking vigour, interest, or imagination; dull
meanings.example: Critics dismissed the sequel as an insipid retread of the original's plot.

etymology: From Late Latin insipidus, from in- 'not' + sapidus 'having flavour', from sapere 'to taste'

meanings.definition: Showing a casual lack of concern; carefree and nonchalant
meanings.example: She strolled through the crisis with an insouciant grace that both impressed and infuriated her colleagues.

etymology: From French insouciant, from in- 'not' + souciant 'worrying', from soucier 'to worry'

meanings.definition: A violent uprising against an authority or government
meanings.example: The insurrection was quelled within days, but its political aftershocks lasted for years.

meanings.definition: An act or instance of organized resistance to an established authority
meanings.example: Historians now view the failed insurrection as a turning point in the colony's struggle for independence.

etymology: From Latin insurrectionem, from insurgere 'to rise up', from in- 'upon' + surgere 'to rise'

meanings.definition: Showing a lack of self-control or moderation, especially in the consumption of alcohol
meanings.example: His intemperate drinking habits eventually cost him both his career and his marriage.

meanings.definition: Given to or characterised by excessive or immoderate behaviour or language
meanings.example: The senator's intemperate outburst on the floor drew swift condemnation from both parties.

etymology: From Latin intemperatus 'immoderate', from in- 'not' + temperatus 'restrained'

meanings.definition: Having a particular habit, activity, or interest that is long-established and unlikely to change
meanings.example: An inveterate traveller, she had visited over sixty countries before turning forty.

meanings.definition: (Of a feeling or habit) firmly and deeply established; ingrained
meanings.example: The community's inveterate distrust of outsiders made integration a slow process.

etymology: From Latin inveteratus 'of long standing', past participle of inveterare 'to make old'

meanings.definition: Impossible to deny or disprove; indisputable
meanings.example: The forensic evidence presented by the prosecution was irrefutable, leaving the jury with little room for doubt.

meanings.definition: Too strong or convincing to be argued against
meanings.example: She built an irrefutable case for restructuring the department based on three years of performance data.

etymology: From Late Latin irrefutabilis, from in- 'not' + refutare 'to refute'

meanings.definition: Tearful or given to weeping
meanings.example: The lachrymose widow could barely deliver her eulogy without breaking down.

meanings.definition: Inducing or tending to induce tears; mournful
meanings.example: The film's lachrymose final act divided audiences between those who found it moving and those who thought it manipulative.

etymology: From Latin lacrimosus 'tearful', from lacrima 'tear'

meanings.definition: Expressing praise and commendation
meanings.example: The laudatory review in the national press propelled the debut novel onto the bestseller list.

meanings.definition: Serving to praise or extol the virtues of someone or something
meanings.example: Her laudatory speech at the retirement dinner recounted decades of public service.

etymology: From Latin laudatorius, from laudare 'to praise', from laus 'praise'

meanings.definition: Inefficient or inept; clumsy in handling situations
meanings.example: The diplomat's maladroit handling of the negotiations nearly derailed the entire peace process.

meanings.definition: Lacking physical coordination or dexterity
meanings.example: His maladroit attempts at assembling the furniture left a trail of mismatched parts.

etymology: From French maladroit, from mal 'badly' + adroit 'skilful'

meanings.definition: Having or showing a wish to do evil to others; deliberately harmful
meanings.example: The malevolent intent behind the anonymous letters became clear only after the police investigation.

meanings.definition: Having a harmful or threatening influence
meanings.example: A malevolent force seemed to pervade the abandoned house, unsettling even the most sceptical visitors.

etymology: From Latin malevolentem, from male 'badly' + volens 'wishing', from velle 'to wish'

meanings.definition: A person who dislikes humankind and avoids human society
meanings.example: After years of corporate betrayal, he retreated to a mountain cabin and became a confirmed misanthrope.

meanings.definition: A person who habitually distrusts the motives or sincerity of others
meanings.example: The novel's protagonist is a misanthrope whose cynical worldview is gradually challenged by unexpected kindness.

etymology: From Greek misanthropos, from misein 'to hate' + anthropos 'human being'

meanings.definition: Stubbornly refusing to change one's opinion or chosen course of action despite attempts to persuade
meanings.example: The obstinate negotiator refused every compromise, ultimately causing the deal to collapse.

meanings.definition: (Of a condition) very difficult to deal with or overcome
meanings.example: The obstinate stain on the marble countertop resisted every cleaning agent they tried.

etymology: From Latin obstinatus 'resolved, determined', past participle of obstinare 'to persist'

meanings.definition: Annoyingly insensitive or slow to understand
meanings.example: He was being deliberately obtuse, pretending not to grasp the implications of his own argument.

meanings.definition: (Of an angle) greater than 90° and less than 180°
meanings.example: The architect incorporated obtuse angles into the roof design to maximise natural light.

etymology: From Latin obtusus 'blunted, dull', past participle of obtundere 'to beat against'

meanings.definition: Characterised by vulgar or pretentious display; designed to impress or attract notice
meanings.example: The oligarch's ostentatious yacht, complete with a helipad and gold fixtures, drew scorn from the press.

meanings.definition: Making an excessive or conspicuous display of wealth, knowledge, or skill
meanings.example: Her ostentatious use of obscure jargon alienated rather than impressed the audience.

etymology: From Latin ostentare 'to display', frequentative of ostendere 'to show'

meanings.definition: The presence of something in only small or insufficient quantities; scarcity
meanings.example: The paucity of reliable data made it impossible to draw firm conclusions from the study.

meanings.definition: An inadequacy or dearth of something needed
meanings.example: Critics lamented the paucity of affordable housing options in the rapidly gentrifying neighbourhood.

etymology: From Latin paucitas, from paucus 'few'

meanings.definition: Excessively concerned with minor details or rules, especially in teaching or academic contexts
meanings.example: The pedantic lecturer spent twenty minutes correcting trivial citation formatting instead of discussing the argument.

meanings.definition: Ostentatiously displaying academic knowledge or overly precise about trivial matters
meanings.example: His pedantic insistence on etymological accuracy at dinner parties made him a tiresome conversationalist.

etymology: From Italian pedante 'teacher', possibly from Latin paedagogare 'to teach'

meanings.definition: Holding firmly to an opinion or a course of action despite difficulty or opposition
meanings.example: The pertinacious journalist spent three years pursuing the corruption story before finally publishing it.

meanings.definition: Stubbornly unyielding or tenacious
meanings.example: Only the most pertinacious weeds survived the gardener's relentless campaign of removal.

etymology: From Latin pertinax 'holding fast', from per- 'thoroughly' + tenax 'tenacious'

meanings.definition: Affectedly and irritatingly grand, solemn, or self-important
meanings.example: The pompous bureaucrat lectured everyone on protocol while ignoring the substantive issue at hand.

meanings.definition: Characterised by excessive stateliness or ceremony; grandiose
meanings.example: The pompous architectural style of the new civic building was widely mocked as a waste of public funds.

etymology: From Old French pompeux, from Late Latin pomposus, from Latin pompa 'procession, pomp'

meanings.definition: To cause an event or situation, typically an unwelcome one, to happen suddenly or prematurely
meanings.example: The CEO's abrupt resignation precipitated a sharp decline in the company's share price.

meanings.definition: To cause a substance to be deposited in solid form from a solution
meanings.example: Adding the reagent precipitated fine white crystals at the bottom of the flask.

meanings.definition: Done, made, or acting suddenly or without careful consideration (adjective use)
meanings.example: His precipitate decision to sell the business without consulting partners led to costly litigation.

etymology: From Latin praecipitare 'to throw headlong', from praeceps 'headfirst', from prae 'before' + caput 'head'

meanings.definition: To prevent from happening; make impossible
meanings.example: The severe weather precluded any possibility of launching the satellite on schedule.

meanings.definition: To exclude or rule out in advance
meanings.example: Her lack of formal qualifications did not preclude her from being considered for the senior role.

etymology: From Latin praecludere 'to shut off', from prae 'before' + claudere 'to close'

meanings.definition: Relating to or denoting an animal that preys on others
meanings.example: The predatory instincts of the barn owl make it an effective controller of rodent populations.

meanings.definition: Seeking to exploit or oppress others for personal gain
meanings.example: Consumer groups condemned the company's predatory lending practices that targeted vulnerable borrowers.

etymology: From Latin praedatorius 'plundering', from praedator 'plunderer', from praeda 'prey'

meanings.definition: Having or showing knowledge of events before they take place; far-sighted
meanings.example: Her prescient investment in renewable energy stocks a decade ago proved remarkably profitable.

meanings.definition: Displaying an uncanny awareness of future developments
meanings.example: The author's prescient portrayal of mass surveillance now reads more like journalism than fiction.

etymology: From Latin praescientem, from praescire 'to know beforehand', from prae 'before' + scire 'to know'

meanings.definition: To speak or act in an evasive way; to avoid giving a direct answer
meanings.example: The witness continued to prevaricate under cross-examination, never quite answering the question asked.

meanings.definition: To deliberately delay or avoid committing to a course of action
meanings.example: The committee prevaricated for months, unwilling to take responsibility for the unpopular decision.

etymology: From Latin praevaricari 'to walk crookedly, deviate', from prae 'before' + varicare 'to straddle'

meanings.definition: The quality of having strong moral principles; honesty and decency
meanings.example: Her reputation for financial probity made her the obvious choice to chair the audit committee.

meanings.definition: Uprightness of character and conduct, especially in professional matters
meanings.example: The judiciary's probity is the cornerstone of public trust in the legal system.

etymology: From Latin probitas 'goodness, uprightness', from probus 'good, virtuous'

meanings.definition: Recklessly extravagant or wasteful in the use of resources
meanings.example: The profligate spending of the previous administration left the treasury virtually empty.

meanings.definition: Shamelessly immoral or debauched
meanings.example: The biography paints a portrait of a profligate aristocrat whose excesses scandalised polite society.

etymology: From Latin profligatus 'ruined, dissolute', past participle of profligare 'to overthrow'

meanings.definition: Producing a great number or amount of something, especially works of art or literature
meanings.example: The prolific novelist published over forty books in a career spanning five decades.

meanings.definition: Present in large numbers or quantities; plentiful
meanings.example: The region's prolific wildflower displays attract thousands of visitors each spring.

etymology: From Medieval Latin prolificus, from Latin proles 'offspring' + facere 'to make'

meanings.definition: Giving or indicating a good chance of success; favourable
meanings.example: The calm seas and clear skies provided propitious conditions for the maiden voyage.

meanings.definition: Favourably disposed toward someone or something; benevolent
meanings.example: Fortune proved propitious to the young entrepreneur, whose first venture exceeded all projections.

etymology: From Latin propitius 'favourable, gracious', perhaps from pro- 'forward' + petere 'to seek'

meanings.definition: To put forward an idea, theory, or point of view for consideration by others
meanings.example: Darwin propounded his theory of natural selection in On the Origin of Species.

meanings.definition: To formally present a legal document, especially a will, for official validation
meanings.example: The executor propounded the deceased's will before the probate court.

etymology: Alteration of earlier propone, from Latin proponere 'to set forth', from pro- 'forward' + ponere 'to put'

meanings.definition: Having or using the style or diction of prose as opposed to poetry; lacking poetic beauty
meanings.example: The translation, while accurate, rendered the verse in prosaic language that stripped away its musicality.

meanings.definition: Commonplace; unromantic; dull and uninspiring
meanings.example: Beneath the company's visionary mission statement lay the prosaic reality of cost-cutting and layoffs.

etymology: From Late Latin prosaicus, from Latin prosa 'straightforward discourse', from prorsus 'direct'

meanings.definition: Having or encouraging an excessive interest in sexual matters
meanings.example: The censor objected to what she described as the novel's prurient depictions of illicit relationships.

meanings.definition: Characterised by an unwholesome or morbid curiosity
meanings.example: The tabloid catered to the public's prurient fascination with celebrity scandals.

etymology: From Latin prurientem, present participle of prurire 'to itch, to long for'

meanings.definition: Eager or quick to argue, quarrel, or fight; combative
meanings.example: The pugnacious defence attorney challenged every piece of evidence with relentless vigour.

meanings.definition: Having a naturally aggressive or belligerent disposition
meanings.example: Despite his small stature, the pugnacious terrier never backed down from a confrontation.

etymology: From Latin pugnax 'combative', from pugnare 'to fight', from pugnus 'fist'

meanings.definition: Showing a lack of courage or determination; timid and cowardly
meanings.example: The board's pusillanimous refusal to confront the CEO allowed the misconduct to continue unchecked.

meanings.definition: Proceeding from or indicating a cowardly spirit
meanings.example: His pusillanimous retreat from the public debate was widely seen as an admission of defeat.

etymology: From Late Latin pusillanimis, from Latin pusillus 'very small' + animus 'mind, spirit'

meanings.definition: A state of perplexity or uncertainty over what to do in a difficult situation; a dilemma
meanings.example: The journalist faced a quandary: publish the story and betray a source, or suppress it and fail the public.

meanings.definition: A difficult or complicated situation from which there is no obvious escape
meanings.example: The government found itself in a fiscal quandary, needing to cut spending without reducing essential services.

etymology: Origin uncertain; possibly from Latin quando 'when', or a dialectal English formation

meanings.definition: Aggressively greedy or grasping, especially for money or resources
meanings.example: The rapacious landlord raised rents every quarter, driving out long-standing tenants.

meanings.definition: (Of an animal) living by preying on other animals; predatory
meanings.example: Rapacious gulls descended on the fishing boats as soon as the nets were hauled aboard.

etymology: From Latin rapax 'grasping', from rapere 'to seize'

meanings.definition: Stubbornly resistant to authority, control, or treatment
meanings.example: The refractory patient refused to follow the prescribed medication regimen despite repeated warnings.

meanings.definition: Resistant to heat or a process of manufacture; hard to melt or work
meanings.example: Refractory bricks line the interior of blast furnaces to withstand extreme temperatures.

etymology: From Latin refractarius 'stubborn', from refragari 'to oppose'

meanings.definition: To consign or dismiss to an inferior rank or position
meanings.example: After the restructuring, she was relegated to a minor administrative role with no decision-making authority.

meanings.definition: To assign to a particular class or category, typically a lower one
meanings.example: Climate policy was relegated to a footnote in the party's election manifesto.

etymology: From Latin relegare 'to send away, remove', from re- 'back' + legare 'to send'

meanings.definition: To make a forceful or earnest protest or objection
meanings.example: Several board members remonstrated against the proposal to slash the research budget.

meanings.definition: To reason earnestly with someone in opposition to their actions or views
meanings.example: The teacher remonstrated gently with the student about the importance of meeting deadlines.

etymology: From Medieval Latin remonstrare 'to demonstrate', from re- 'again' + monstrare 'to show'

meanings.definition: An unprincipled or immoral person; a scoundrel
meanings.example: The charming reprobate had swindled half the town before anyone caught on to his schemes.

meanings.definition: A person rejected by God and beyond redemption (in Calvinist theology)
meanings.example: Puritan sermons frequently warned congregants of the fate awaiting the unrepentant reprobate.

etymology: From Latin reprobatus 'disapproved', past participle of reprobare 'to reject'

meanings.definition: A quick, clever reply to an insult or criticism; a sharp retort
meanings.example: Her riposte to the senator's accusation drew appreciative laughter from the press gallery.

meanings.definition: A quick return thrust in fencing following a parry
meanings.example: The fencer's lightning riposte caught her opponent off guard and scored the decisive point.

etymology: From French riposte, from Italian risposta 'response', from rispondere 'to respond'

meanings.definition: To think deeply about something; to meditate or reflect at length
meanings.example: She spent the weekend ruminating on whether to accept the overseas posting or stay close to family.

meanings.definition: To chew the cud, as a ruminant animal does
meanings.example: The cattle ruminated placidly in the meadow as the afternoon sun warmed the hillside.

etymology: From Latin ruminari 'to chew over again', from rumen 'throat, gullet'

meanings.definition: Having or showing keen mental discernment and good judgement; shrewd and wise
meanings.example: The sagacious investor divested from the sector months before the market correction.

meanings.definition: Demonstrating foresight and perceptive understanding
meanings.example: Her sagacious advice during the merger negotiations saved the company from an unfavourable deal.

etymology: From Latin sagax 'wise, prophetic', from sagire 'to perceive keenly'

meanings.definition: Making a show of being morally superior to other people; self-righteously pious
meanings.example: The sanctimonious politician who preached family values was later exposed for embezzling charity funds.

meanings.definition: Excessively or affectedly pious in manner or attitude
meanings.example: His sanctimonious tone during the meeting alienated colleagues who might otherwise have supported his proposal.

etymology: From Latin sanctimonia 'sanctity, sacredness', from sanctus 'holy'

meanings.definition: Grimly mocking or cynical; disdainfully humorous
meanings.example: His sardonic commentary on corporate culture made the keynote speech both uncomfortable and unforgettable.

meanings.definition: Characterised by bitter or scornful derision
meanings.example: She gave a sardonic smile when told that yet another restructuring would improve morale.

etymology: From French sardonique, from Latin sardonius, alteration of Greek sardonios 'bitter, scornful'

meanings.definition: Relating to tailoring, clothes, or style of dress
meanings.example: His sartorial elegance was the product of decades spent curating a wardrobe of bespoke suits.

meanings.definition: Relating to one's personal manner of dressing and overall appearance; pertaining to the aesthetics of attire
meanings.example: Her sartorial choices consistently set trends long before they reached mainstream fashion.

etymology: From Latin sartor 'tailor, patcher', from sarcire 'to patch, mend'

meanings.definition: Diligent, thorough, and extremely attentive to details; painstaking
meanings.example: The archivist's scrupulous cataloguing ensured that every document could be located within minutes.

meanings.definition: Very concerned to avoid doing wrong; guided by a strict regard for what is morally right
meanings.example: She was scrupulous about declaring every gift received in her role as a public official.

etymology: From Latin scrupulosus 'anxious, careful', from scrupulus 'small sharp stone, anxiety'

meanings.definition: A state of temporary suspension or inactivity, pending resolution of circumstances
meanings.example: The lawsuit was held in abeyance while the parties attempted mediation.

meanings.definition: A condition in which something is temporarily not in force or effect
meanings.example: Development plans were kept in abeyance until the environmental impact study was completed.

meanings.definition: A strong creative impulse or inspiration, especially one believed to be of divine or supernatural origin
meanings.example: The composer described the symphony as the product of a sudden afflatus that kept him at his desk through the night.

meanings.definition: A strongly worded criticism, censure, or adverse comment
meanings.example: The reviewer's animadversions on the novel's flawed structure proved persuasive to many readers.

meanings.definition: The act of passing critical or censorious judgment
meanings.example: The board's animadversion against the auditor's findings prompted a full internal inquiry.

meanings.definition: A formal written defence of one's opinions, conduct, or way of life
meanings.example: Newman's Apologia pro Vita Sua remains one of the finest examples of Victorian religious autobiography.

meanings.definition: A reasoned argument or vindication of a particular belief or doctrine
meanings.example: Her memoir reads less as a personal confession than as an apologia for her decision to leave public life.

meanings.definition: A specialized vocabulary or set of idioms used by a particular social group, subculture, or profession
meanings.example: The novel's authenticity depended on the author's precise command of the criminal argot of the Paris underworld.

meanings.definition: Any form of language peculiar to a particular group or trade; jargon
meanings.example: Medical students quickly absorb the argot of the hospital, learning to communicate efficiently with senior clinicians.

meanings.definition: Humorous or witty conversation; good-natured playful banter
meanings.example: The interviewer's easy badinage put the celebrity at ease before the more probing questions began.

meanings.definition: Owing gratitude or moral obligation to someone for help or a benefit received
meanings.example: She felt beholden to her mentor for securing the introduction that launched her entire career.

meanings.definition: Rough and bad-mannered; lacking social graces or refinement
meanings.example: His boorish treatment of service staff was so notorious that several restaurants quietly declined his future reservations.

meanings.definition: Showing a lack of cultural sophistication or sensitivity to social norms
meanings.example: The diplomat's boorish remarks about the host country's cuisine caused a minor diplomatic incident.

meanings.definition: A trite, unoriginal statement or idea; a cliché
meanings.example: The speech was full of political bromides about unity and progress that convinced no one.

meanings.definition: A compound of bromine used historically as a sedative drug
meanings.example: Victorian physicians routinely prescribed bromides as a remedy for nervousness and insomnia.

meanings.definition: To intimidate someone by stern looks or words; to bully someone into doing something
meanings.example: The aggressive barrister was warned by the judge to stop browbeating the witness on the stand.

meanings.definition: To grow or develop rapidly; to flourish
meanings.example: The city's tech sector began to burgeon after several major corporations chose it as their regional headquarters.

meanings.definition: (Of a plant) to put out new buds or shoots; to begin to blossom
meanings.example: By mid-March the hedgerows were beginning to burgeon with the first green shoots of the season.

meanings.definition: To reprimand or censure someone severely
meanings.example: The independent report castigated the government for its dangerously slow response to the humanitarian crisis.

meanings.definition: To chastise or punish with the aim of correcting behaviour
meanings.example: The ancient practice of castigating oneself as an act of penance has largely disappeared from mainstream religious observance.

meanings.definition: Cautiously reluctant; wary or circumspect about doing or giving something
meanings.example: After the failed merger, the board was chary of any further acquisitions without thorough due diligence.

meanings.definition: To engage in casual, informal conversation; to chat
meanings.example: The retired professors would confabulate for hours in the faculty lounge, exchanging memories of past colleagues.

meanings.definition: (Psychology) To unconsciously fill in gaps in memory with fabricated events believed to be genuine
meanings.example: Patients with Korsakoff syndrome often confabulate, unknowingly constructing plausible-sounding accounts of events they cannot recall.

meanings.definition: To secretly allow or tacitly consent to wrongdoing or immoral behaviour
meanings.example: The auditors appeared to have connived at the accounting irregularities for years before the fraud was uncovered.

meanings.definition: To conspire or scheme with others to bring about something
meanings.example: Several board members were found to have connived with the CEO to conceal the full extent of the company's losses.

meanings.definition: The state of feeling deeply remorseful and sorry for wrongdoing; genuine penitence
meanings.example: His public expression of contrition after the scandal did little to restore confidence in his leadership.

meanings.definition: Showing a contemptible lack of courage; cowardly in a morally disgraceful way
meanings.example: The senator's craven refusal to support the amendment drew sharp criticism from within his own party.

meanings.definition: The state of being responsible for a fault or wrong; legal or moral blameworthiness
meanings.example: The investigation sought to determine the degree of culpability of each contractor in the building's collapse.

meanings.definition: Moral guilt or responsibility for an act that causes harm
meanings.example: Questions of moral culpability are central to the philosophical debate about free will and determinism.

meanings.definition: A bad-tempered, cantankerous person, especially one who is easily annoyed by others
meanings.example: Despite his reputation as a curmudgeon, the old editor harboured a fierce loyalty to his junior staff.

meanings.definition: A sudden and complete failure or disaster; a humiliating fiasco
meanings.example: The product launch became a debacle when the app crashed live on stage in front of three thousand journalists.
